claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a protocol similar to REST API or GraphQL that allows communication between MCP servers and clients, enabling standardized message passing.
actionable: Use MCP when building AI integrations that need standardized server-client communication, treating it as a language-agnostic protocol like REST.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: MCP servers have four main components: tools (functions callable by clients), resources (data/files accessible on the server), prompts (pre-created prompts clients can request), and samplings (server requesting information from the client's AI).
actionable: When designing an MCP server, organize functionality into these four categories: use tools for actions, resources for data access, prompts for reusable AI instructions, and samplings for server-to-AI requests.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Tools are the most commonly used MCP component—they are functions on the server that clients can invoke, with parameters defined using schemas (e.g., Zod or JSON Schema).
actionable: Build MCP tools by defining clear parameter schemas (prefer Zod for TypeScript) and descriptions that help AI understand what the tool does and when to use it.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Resources in MCP can be static (like database records or files) or templated (dynamic URIs like 'users/{id}'). Templates enable flexible data access patterns matching URLs.
actionable: Use resource templates (e.g., 'users/{id}') for parameterized data access, allowing clients to fetch specific items by substituting dynamic parameters in the URI.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Prompts in MCP allow servers to define pre-formatted prompts that clients can request and use directly, reducing the need for clients to construct complex prompts themselves.
actionable: Create MCP prompts for complex or domain-specific instructions; clients can request them with minimal parameters and get well-formatted prompts ready for AI.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Samplings (server-initiated requests) reverse the normal client-server flow: the server sends a prompt to the client's AI and waits for the AI's response to use in server logic.
actionable: Use sampling in tools when you need the server to request AI-generated data (e.g., generating fake data); call mcp.request() with sampling/create_message to ask the client's AI.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Two main transport protocols for MCP are standard input/output (for local applications) and HTTP streaming (for remote/web applications); the protocol handles both transparently.
actionable: Choose stdio transport for local MCP servers (same machine as client) and HTTP streaming for remote servers; the SDK abstracts most transport details.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Zod is preferred over JSON Schema for parameter validation in MCP TypeScript servers because it's more ergonomic and easier to work with in code.
actionable: Use Zod for defining tool parameters in TypeScript MCP servers instead of raw JSON Schema for better DX and type safety.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Tool annotations (readOnly, destructive, idempotent, openWorld hints) provide the AI with metadata about side effects and behavior, helping it make better decisions about tool usage.
actionable: Annotate tools with these hints: set readOnly=false for mutating tools, destructive=true if it deletes data (AI will warn users), idempotent=true only if calling multiple times has same effect.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: The MCP Inspector tool (@modelcontextprotocol/inspector) is essential for testing and debugging MCP servers before integrating with AI clients; it provides a browser-based interface to test tools, resources, and prompts.
actionable: Install and use MCP Inspector during development: run 'npm install @modelcontextprotocol/inspector' and create a dev script with 'dangerously_omit_off=true' to avoid token refresh cycles.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: MCP servers return responses with a specific structure: an object containing a 'contents' array of items with 'type' (text/image/etc) and corresponding content properties.
actionable: Always wrap tool and resource responses in {contents: [{type: 'text', text: '...'}, ...]} structure so clients can properly parse and display results.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: VS Code MCP integration allows adding servers via the MCP settings UI; servers can be configured with debug info (debug type, working directory, watch patterns) for better development experience.
actionable: Set up MCP servers in VS Code with Ctrl+Shift+P > 'MCP: Add server' and configure debug settings (type: node, cwd, watch pattern) to enable debugging support.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: MCP clients can dynamically call tools by constructing a tools object that maps tool names to objects with description, parameters schema, and an execute function.
actionable: In MCP clients, use reduce() to build a tools object from server tool arrays, mapping each tool with an execute handler that calls mcp.callTool() when the AI selects that tool.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Resource URIs can contain dynamic parameters in curly braces (e.g., 'users/{id}'); clients must parse these templates and replace parameters with actual values before reading the resource.
actionable: When building MCP clients that read templated resources, use regex to match {paramName} patterns, prompt users for values, then replace parameters in the final URI before calling readResource().
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: Experimental Node.js feature allows importing JSON files dynamically using import() with {assert: {type: 'json'}}, enabling runtime JSON data loading without file system APIs.
actionable: Use dynamic JSON imports in Node.js (await import('./file.json', {assert: {type: 'json'}})) to load data files at runtime cleanly without fs utilities.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch

claim: When building an MCP client that uses an AI model, use the 'ai' library with provider SDKs (e.g., @ai-sdk/google) to abstract provider-specific details and enable tool calling.
actionable: Build MCP clients using 'npm install ai @ai-sdk/google' and call generateText() with a tools object for automatic AI tool selection and execution.
source_title: The Ultimate MCP Crash Course - Build From Scratch
